# Optimization for Data Science


Modern data science depends on optimization. Model fitting often means choosing
parameters that minimize a loss function or maximize an objective.

## Objective functions

An objective function maps a parameter vector $\theta$ to a real number
$L(\theta)$. In regression, $L$ may be a sum of squared errors. In logistic
regression, $L$ may be a negative log-likelihood.

## Derivatives and gradients

For a differentiable function $f : \mathbb{R}^p \to \mathbb{R}$, the gradient is

$$
\nabla f(\theta) =
\left(
\frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta_1},
\ldots,
\frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta_p}
\right)^T.
$$

The gradient points in the direction of steepest increase.

## Gradient descent

To minimize $f$, we can iterate

$$
\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla f(\theta^{(t)}),
$$

where $\eta > 0$ is a learning rate.

## Convexity

Convex functions are attractive because every local minimum is a global minimum.
Many classical statistical problems are convex, while deep learning problems
typically are not.

## R example

```{r}
f <- function(x) (x - 3)^2 + 1
x <- 0
eta <- 0.1
path <- numeric(20)
for (t in 1:20) {
  grad <- 2*(x - 3)
  x <- x - eta*grad
  path[t] <- x
}
path
```

## Exercises

1. Define the gradient of a multivariable function.
2. What role does the learning rate play in gradient descent?
3. Explain the difference between minimization and maximization.
4. Give one reason why feature scaling can help optimization.
